messages.content:
YOUR ROLE: You are the partner running a pre-mortem on this market model for a
paying client. The client's own team has already read every number in the
ledger, including every sensitivity band. Your reputation rests on surfacing
what THEY would not list: risks that look benign on the surface but could kill
the venture, argued mechanistically, anchored in the model.

HARD REQUIREMENTS — a hypothesis that misses any of these will be rejected by
a validator before any human reads it:
1. targetNodes: real ledger node ids; perturbation ops only on FUNNEL-ACTIVE nodes.
2. mechanism: at least 3 steps (trigger → propagation → model impact), EVERY
   step carrying refs — "ledger:<nodeId>", "digest:<group>#c<n>", or
   "profile:<field>". A step you cannot reference is a step you are guessing.
3. falsifier: the single concrete observation that would kill this risk. If you
   cannot name one, it is not a claim.
4. whyMissable: why a competent founder's own risk list omits this. "It's
   non-obvious" is not an argument; name the surface appearance that hides it.
5. likelihood: a probability with a basis ("evidence" | "base-rate" |
   "judgment") and a rationale. No naked numbers.
6. indicators: at least 2 observable early warnings, each with WHERE to watch
   (a named tender portal, an operator's capex call, a standards docket, a
   vendor's price list) and the reading that should trigger re-assessment.
7. mitigations: at least 2. Where the right move is to buy information, use
   type "information" and name the ledger node it de-risks (voiNodeId) — its
   value will be computed, not asserted.

QUOTAS (per lens): at least one hypothesis self-tagged expectedObviousness
"low", and at least one whose perturbation targets NONE of tamBase /
serviceableFactor / obtainableFactor — the tornado already covers those bands;
look past them.

MINE THE RESIDUALS: the highest-yield material is where sources disagree with
the curated value (the digests' "tensions"), where searches came back empty
(the digests' "absences" — an unquantified market is a risk, not a comfort),
where a derivation's crossCheck diverges from its primary method, and where a
precise-looking value carries maturity "needs-source".

HOW YOUR CLAIM BECOMES A NUMBER (the perturbation contract):
- You never state € impacts. You emit machine-readable ops; the engine computes
  ΔTAM/ΔSAM/ΔYAM by re-running the real funnel. Any € figure you write in prose
  will be stripped.
- Ops: {nodeId, op: "set"|"scale"|"add", value} rewrite one leaf's value;
  {nodeId, op: "exclude"} removes a dimension cell from the venture's reachable
  scope ("unavailable to us" — foreclosure), which is different from shrinking it.
- Ops apply in array order. Ratio nodes must stay inside [0,1]; tamBase must stay > 0.
- Only FUNNEL-ACTIVE nodes may be perturbed (marked in the ledger you receive).
  shape.cagr and shape.cr3 are context — a demand-pace or concentration risk must
  be translated into the funnel nodes it would actually move (tamBase level,
  serviceableFactor reach, obtainableFactor pace, or a share).
- Sibling shares are NOT re-normalized when you move one share (documented v1
  simplification). If you mean a mix-SHIFT, perturb multiple siblings; if you
  mean "this slice is smaller in absolute terms", one op is correct.
- Perturbing an assumption leaf overrides whatever the user set: your claim is
  about the world, not about their slider.

BANNED GENRES (emitting one of these wastes your slot):
- "Find 2 customers to talk to and get an LOI" — an action item, not a risk. It names no node, no mechanism, no falsifier.
- "Do research on customer buying behaviour" — a homework assignment any consultant could write for any company.
- "Competition is strong / the market is concentrated" — the ledger already states CR3. Restating a fact is not a risk; the risk is a MECHANISM that concentration enables.
- "The market may be smaller than expected" — the tamBase band (240–520) already says this. Only worth saying if you name WHICH derivation hop fails and WHY.
- "Regulatory changes could impact the business" — name the instrument, the compliance artifact it requires, and the date it lands, or drop it.
- "The band's low end might be true" with no new reason — the band is already in the model; you are paid for what is NOT in the model.

GOLD EXEMPLAR (the target register — do NOT repeat its content):
{
 "id": "busway-hollowing-hyperscale",
 "title": "Busway + OCP power shelves hollow out the hyperscale 44% from inside the scope boundary",
 "narrative": "The model excludes busbars and power shelves as out-of-scope substitutes — a scoping footnote. But hyperscale is 44% of TAM, and it is precisely the segment whose new AI-density builds are moving power distribution from rack PDUs to overhead busway feeding OCP-style in-rack power shelves. If that architecture becomes the default for new CE hyperscale capacity, the largest slice of this TAM is not 'ours to win slowly' — it is structurally shrinking while the headline number stays flat. The exclusion list quietly became the market thesis.",
 "category": "boundary",
 "targetNodes": [
  "seg.hyperscale"
 ],
 "mechanism": [
  {
   "step": "trigger",
   "text": "New hyperscale AI-density builds adopt busway + in-rack DC power shelves instead of discrete rack PDUs",
   "refs": [
    "digest:seg-mix#c?",
    "ledger:seg.hyperscale"
   ]
  },
  {
   "step": "propagation",
   "text": "CE hyperscale capacity additions (the growth the 9% CAGR is made of) carry falling rack-PDU content per MW; replacement demand in legacy halls does not offset it",
   "refs": [
    "ledger:shape.cagr",
    "digest:tam-base#c?"
   ]
  },
  {
   "step": "model-impact",
   "text": "The hyperscale share of the rack-PDU market shrinks in absolute terms — seg.hyperscale 0.44 overstates the addressable slice for a new entrant's window",
   "refs": [
    "ledger:seg.hyperscale"
   ]
  }
 ],
 "whyMissable": "Substitutes were explicitly considered and excluded — the boundary decision looks handled. The kill path is not 'we misjudged the boundary' but 'the boundary moved into the biggest segment while the TAM base year stayed put'.",
 "expectedObviousness": "low",
 "falsifier": "2025–26 CE hyperscale fit-out specs (or Schneider/Vertiv rack-power order mixes) showing intelligent rack PDUs holding share in new AI-density halls.",
 "likelihood": {
  "value": 0.35,
  "rationale": "OCP power-shelf adoption is documented for AI racks, but CE colocation and enterprise retrofits (56% of TAM) keep buying rack PDUs; timing within a 12-month window is uncertain.",
  "basis": "evidence"
 },
 "perturbation": [
  {
   "nodeId": "seg.hyperscale",
   "op": "set",
   "value": 0.22,
   "note": "≈half of the hyperscale slice migrates to busway/shelf architectures"
  }
 ],
 "indicators": [
  {
   "signal": "Rack-power architecture specified in new CE hyperscale tenders (busway/shelf vs PDU)",
   "where": "Frankfurt/Amsterdam data-center fit-out tenders; OCP regional summit deployment talks",
   "threshold": "≥2 flagship CE builds specifying shelf-based distribution",
   "updates": "increases"
  },
  {
   "signal": "Incumbent rack-PDU revenue mix commentary (Vertiv/Schneider earnings)",
   "where": "Quarterly earnings calls, rack-power product-line commentary",
   "threshold": "PDU line flat while busway line grows double-digit",
   "updates": "increases"
  }
 ],
 "mitigations": [
  {
   "action": "Commission a CE rack-power architecture survey across the 2026 hyperscale build pipeline",
   "type": "information",
   "voiNodeId": "seg.hyperscale"
  },
  {
   "action": "Qualify the product line for colocation/enterprise retrofit first, where PDU form-factor is sticky",
   "type": "strategic"
  }
 ]
}

content: {"hypotheses":[{"id":"colo-follows-hyperscale-spec","title":"Wholesale colocation inherits hyperscale rack-power specs — the 'safe' 18% slice buys what its tenants tell it to","narrative":"The model treats colocation (seg.colocation, 0.18) as an independent PDU-buying segment — the sticky, PDU-friendly counterweight to hyperscale substitution risk. But CBRE reports that European colo take-up is being driven by hyperscalers and AI/HPC providers leasing wholesale capacity, and in wholesale deals the tenant specifies the rack-power architecture, not the landlord. If hyperscale tenants standardize on busway + power-shelf fit-outs in their leased suites, the substitution the boundary excluded walks through the colocation door: the colo slice's PDU content per MW falls in lockstep with hyperscale's, while the segment share in the model sits untouched — and seg.colocation carries no sensitivity band at all to absorb it.","category":"boundary","targetNodes":["seg.colocation"],"mechanism":[{"step":"trigger","text":"CBRE reports European colocation take-up outstripping new supply, driven specifically by hyperscalers and AI/HPC providers — i.e., the marginal colo MW is a hyperscale-tenant MW, leased wholesale with tenant-specified fit-out","refs":["digest:seg-mix#c24","digest:seg-mix#c15"]},{"step":"propagation","text":"Hyperscale tenants port their own rack-power standards (busway feeds, OCP-style in-rack shelves) into leased suites; colo landlords fit out to tenant spec, so the substitution path excluded at the boundary propagates into the colocation segment via the lease contract, not the landlord's preference","refs":["digest:seg-mix#c14","ledger:seg.colocation"]},{"step":"model-impact","text":"seg.colocation's 0.18 rests on a single Synergy global capacity series (20–22% across vintages) with no sensitivityRange; the model has zero mechanism to register falling PDU content per colo MW, so the colocation slice is overstated in absolute PDU-euro terms during exactly the venture's entry window","refs":["ledger:seg.colocation","digest:seg-mix#c1","digest:seg-mix#c7"]}],"whyMissable":"Colocation is the segment founders point to as the hedge against hyperscale architecture risk — multi-tenant, heterogeneous, PDU-native. That framing assumes the landlord chooses the rack power. In wholesale colo (the part that is actually growing, per CBRE), the tenant chooses — and the tenant is the hyperscaler the hedge was supposed to protect against.","expectedObviousness":"low","falsifier":"Fit-out specifications or tenant BOMs from 2026 wholesale colo deals in Frankfurt/Amsterdam showing intelligent rack PDUs specified in hyperscale-leased suites at rates comparable to retail colo.","likelihood":{"value":0.3,"rationale":"CBRE documents hyperscale-driven colo absorption in Europe, and tenant-specified fit-out is standard in wholesale leases; but retail colocation and enterprise-tenant suites remain genuinely PDU-native, capping how much of the 0.18 converts within 12 months.","basis":"evidence"},"perturbation":[{"nodeId":"seg.colocation","op":"set","value":0.12,"note":"Wholesale hyperscale-leased suites (~1/3 of the colo slice) adopt tenant busway/shelf specs; retail colo stays PDU-native"}],"indicators":[{"signal":"Rack-power architecture named in wholesale colo fit-out specs and tenant technical requirements","where":"TED (EU tenders electronic daily) and TenderNed for NL/DE data-center fit-out packages; Equinix and Digital Realty capex/earnings calls (xScale / hyperscale JV commentary)","threshold":"≥2 CE wholesale deals where tenant spec mandates busway or shelf-based distribution in leased suites","updates":"increases"},{"signal":"Colo landlords marketing 'OCP-ready' or shelf-compatible white space in CE","where":"Dutch Data Center Association annual report; Frankfurt operator (e.g., NTT, CyrusOne, Vantage) product announcements and OCP Regional Summit EMEA talks","threshold":"First CE colo operator advertising OCP-ready suites as standard product","updates":"increases"},{"signal":"Retail vs wholesale mix in CE colo absorption","where":"CBRE European Data Centres quarterly reports","threshold":"Wholesale/hyperscale-leased share of CE take-up exceeding ~60%","updates":"increases"}],"mitigations":[{"action":"Interview 5 CE wholesale colo design/fit-out leads on who specifies rack power in leased suites and what share of 2026 pipeline is tenant-specified","type":"information","voiNodeId":"seg.colocation","note":"Directly replaces the unbanded single-source 0.18 with a spec-controlled vs landlord-controlled split"},{"action":"Position the product for retail colo and enterprise-tenant suites first, with an explicit OCP-compatible roadmap (shelf-integrated metering module) to follow tenant specs rather than fight them","type":"strategic","voiNodeId":null,"note":"Converts the substitution wave into an adjacency rather than a foreclosure"}]},{"id":"enterprise-gpu-pods-skip-pdus","title":"The only growing part of the enterprise 29% ships as pre-integrated GPU racks with shelf power — the venture addresses only the shrinking remainder","narrative":"seg.enterprise (0.29) looks like the conservative, PDU-sticky base: on-prem halls refreshing rack power on slow cycles. But Synergy is explicit that enterprise capacity was flat for years and is only now growing again 'thanks to GenAI applications and GPU infrastructure' — and that increment arrives as OEM-integrated GPU pods built to vendor reference designs with in-rack power shelves, not as field-installed PDUs. Meanwhile the legacy stock the PDU thesis actually addresses is the part Synergy shows declining two percentage points a year toward 19% by 2031. The model books the whole 0.29 as PDU-addressable; structurally, only the shrinking half is.","category":"model-structure","targetNodes":["seg.enterprise"],"mechanism":[{"step":"trigger","text":"Synergy: after a sustained period of no growth, on-premise capacity is getting a boost from GenAI/GPU infrastructure — the enterprise segment's growth is exclusively AI-density capacity","refs":["digest:seg-mix#c20","digest:seg-mix#c2"]},{"step":"propagation","text":"Enterprise AI capacity is procured as pre-integrated racks (vendor reference designs with busbar/power-shelf distribution) from OEMs, bypassing the discrete rack-PDU purchase entirely; the residual PDU-buying legacy stock is the component falling ~2pp/year toward 19% share by 2031","refs":["digest:seg-mix#c22","digest:seg-mix#c5"]},{"step":"model-impact","text":"seg.enterprise 0.29 is a single-source capacity share with no sensitivityRange and no decomposition into legacy-refresh vs GPU-pod capacity; treating all of it as PDU-addressable overstates the enterprise slice a new PDU entrant can sell into during Year 1","refs":["ledger:seg.enterprise","digest:seg-mix#c3"]}],"whyMissable":"The enterprise share is the model's most 'boring' number — a declining legacy segment nobody expects surprises from. The trap is that its headline share is being propped up by exactly the capacity type (GPU pods) that doesn't buy the product, so the stable-looking 0.29 conceals a faster-shrinking addressable core.","expectedObviousness":"medium","falsifier":"CE enterprise DC refresh data (e.g., a Vertiv/Schneider channel survey or distributor sell-through) showing intelligent rack PDUs attached to new enterprise AI/GPU deployments at rates comparable to general-purpose IT racks.","likelihood":{"value":0.35,"rationale":"Synergy's GenAI-boost claim and the 2pp/yr on-prem decline are both sourced; the open question is what fraction of CE enterprise AI capacity ships pre-integrated vs stick-built — documented for US hyperscale-adjacent deployments, thinner evidence for mid-size European enterprises.","basis":"evidence"},"perturbation":[{"nodeId":"seg.enterprise","op":"set","value":0.21,"note":"GPU-pod increment (~1/4 of the enterprise slice) carries no discrete PDU content; legacy refresh continues its documented decline"}],"indicators":[{"signal":"Form factor of enterprise AI infrastructure orders (integrated rack-scale systems vs component builds)","where":"Dell/HPE/Lenovo quarterly earnings — AI server segment commentary on rack-scale vs unit shipments; NVIDIA reference-design partner announcements","threshold":"Rack-scale integrated systems exceeding ~50% of enterprise AI infrastructure revenue mix","updates":"increases"},{"signal":"PDU attach rate in CE enterprise channel sell-through","where":"CE IT distribution (also/ALSO, Ingram Micro) product-category reporting; Schneider/Vertiv earnings commentary on enterprise vs cloud PDU demand","threshold":"Enterprise PDU line flat-to-down while enterprise AI server line grows double-digit","updates":"increases"}],"mitigations":[{"action":"Commission a decomposition of CE enterprise capacity additions into legacy-refresh vs pre-integrated AI-pod capacity (analyst inquiry with Synergy/Omdia plus 5 CIO interviews)","type":"information","voiNodeId":"seg.enterprise","note":"Splits the 0.29 into PDU-addressable and non-addressable components"},{"action":"Target the enterprise refresh cycle explicitly (retrofit-friendly form factors, drop-in replacement for installed Schneider/Vertiv units) rather than new-build AI capacity","type":"strategic","voiNodeId":null,"note":"Aligns Year-1 pipeline with the segment component that verifiably buys PDUs"}]},{"id":"psu-shelf-cagr-divergence-erodes-tambase","title":"The model's own cross-check source is measuring the substitution: PDU+PSU spend growing 21% while rack-PDU grows 9% means the PDU share of rack power is falling every year","narrative":"tamBase's high-end cross-check takes BIS/R&M's Europe PDUs+PSUs figure ($2.45B, 2024) and applies a static ~55% PDU share to get the €495M ceiling. But look at the growth rates the ledger itself carries: the combined PDUs+PSUs category is forecast at 21.23% CAGR while rack-PDU-only sources cluster at 9–9.7%. Arithmetic forces the gap into the non-PDU component — PSUs and power shelves, the very substitutes the boundary excludes — which must be growing at roughly 30%+ to reconcile the two series. That divergence is not a data quirk; it is the substitution trend expressed in euros. A static 55% PDU share is wrong in the direction that matters: it decays each year of the venture's window, dragging both the cross-check ceiling and the curated €320M toward the band's low end even if data-center construction booms.","category":"model-structure","targetNodes":["tamBase"],"mechanism":[{"step":"trigger","text":"BIS/R&M forecast the Europe PDUs+PSUs market at 21.23% CAGR ($2.45B→$20.05B by 2035), while rack-PDU-only forecasts cluster at 9.7% (GVR) — a 2.2x growth-rate gap within overlapping scopes","refs":["digest:tam-base#c2","digest:tam-base#c3","digest:tam-base#c0"]},{"step":"propagation","text":"The growth gap is mathematically absorbed by the PSU/power-shelf component of the combined category — the excluded substitutes — meaning the PDU value share of European rack power declines every year; the ledger's cross-check applies a static ~55% PDU share to derive the €495M ceiling","refs":["ledger:tamBase","digest:tam-base#c4"]},{"step":"model-impact","text":"Both the €495M high edge and the curated €320M assume PDU share of rack-power spend holds through the sizing window; if the share is decaying at the rate the two CAGRs imply, the effective 2026-27 PDU-only base sits below the curated value and the 240–520 band is skewed high, not symmetric","refs":["ledger:tamBase","digest:tam-base#c5"]}],"whyMissable":"The cross-check looks like belt-and-braces diligence — two independent chains landing in one band. Nobody re-reads the two sources' CAGRs against each other, because CAGR is filed under 'growth outlook' (shape.cagr, not funnel-active) rather than 'base-year scope decay'. The tension hides in a ratio between two numbers that are both individually in the model.","expectedObviousness":"medium","falsifier":"The IndexBox EU Rack PDUs report (or Omdia's 2024-base rack-PDU tracker) showing the PDU share of European rack-power/PSU spend stable or rising 2024–2027.","likelihood":{"value":0.4,"rationale":"The CAGR divergence is in the model's own attached evidence and is arithmetically unavoidable; the uncertainty is whether BIS's 21.23% is inflated (aggressive forecast) rather than a true substitution signal — publisher spread on adjacent categories (>2x) means moderate confidence, not high.","basis":"evidence"},"perturbation":[{"nodeId":"tamBase","op":"set","value":265,"note":"PDU share of the combined rack-power category decays ~3-4pp/yr from the assumed 55%, pulling the effective PDU-only base below the curated midpoint"}],"indicators":[{"signal":"Revenue mix between rack-PDU and PSU/power-shelf product lines at incumbents","where":"Vertiv quarterly earnings (critical power segment detail); Delta Electronics and LiteOn investor materials (OCP power-shelf suppliers)","threshold":"Shelf/PSU lines growing >2x the PDU line for two consecutive quarters","updates":"increases"},{"signal":"Updated rack-PDU-only vs PDU+PSU base-year figures at next report vintages","where":"Grand View Research rack-PDU update (2026 vintage); Research and Markets/BIS Europe PDUs+PSUs update","threshold":"Implied PDU share of the combined category dropping below 50%","updates":"increases"}],"mitigations":[{"action":"Purchase the IndexBox EU Rack PDUs report ($4,000) to pin the PDU-only European base directly and remove dependence on the static 55% share assumption","type":"information","voiNodeId":"tamBase","note":"Named in the ledger's own promotion path; also resolves the Europe≈30% unsourced regional split"},{"action":"Re-run the sizing annually with a time-varying PDU-share parameter instead of a static split, so substitution decay is visible in the funnel rather than hidden in the band","type":"operational","voiNodeId":null,"note":"Cheap model change that converts a silent skew into a tracked assumption"}]},{"id":"nl-selfbuild-hotspot-hollows-geo","title":"The Netherlands is CE's hyperscale self-build hotspot — its 28% geography weight counts MW that carry the least PDU content in the region","narrative":"The geo split apportions tamBase by colo MW as if every megawatt carries equal PDU spend. But CBRE places ~60% of Europe's operational hyperscaler self-build capacity in Ireland, the Netherlands, Sweden and Belgium — and NL is the only CE-7 country on that list. Self-build halls are precisely where busway + OCP power-shelf architectures are set as default, and Amsterdam (70% of NL colo) is where those same hyperscale tenants lease wholesale suites. So the NL slice is disproportionately composed of substitution-leading capacity, while Germany's mix skews toward colo/enterprise halls that still buy PDUs. The model's 0.28 NL weight silently assumes PDU intensity is geography-neutral; in CE it is not, and the error concentrates in the second-largest geography node.","category":"boundary","targetNodes":["geo.NL"],"mechanism":[{"step":"trigger","text":"CBRE: ~60% of Europe's operational hyperscaler self-build capacity is located in Ireland, Netherlands, Sweden and Belgium (Q4 2025) — NL is the sole CE-7 member, and European self-build is growing 24% YoY, faster than colocation's 19%","refs":["digest:seg-mix#c16","digest:seg-mix#c14","digest:seg-mix#c15"]},{"step":"propagation","text":"Self-build hyperscale halls are the architecture-setting sites for busway/shelf distribution; combined with Amsterdam's ~70% concentration of NL colo capacity absorbed heavily by hyperscale tenants, NL MW carry systematically lower discrete-PDU content per MW than DE or CH MW","refs":["ledger:geo.NL","digest:seg-mix#c24"]},{"step":"model-impact","text":"geo.NL's 0.28 is derived purely from MW apportionment (951/3,451) with a band (0.24–0.31) that only reflects capacity-count uncertainty, not PDU-intensity variation; the PDU-euro share of NL is below its capacity share, overstating the NL slice of tamBase in absolute terms","refs":["ledger:geo.NL","ledger:tamBase"]}],"whyMissable":"The geo nodes look like the model's best-sourced layer — two capacity methods, triangulated, tight bands. The hidden assumption is not in the MW counts but in the apportionment method itself: euros-per-MW is treated as constant across countries, and no source in the ledger even measures it. A team auditing the MW figures would confirm them and move on.","expectedObviousness":"low","falsifier":"Vendor sell-through or fit-out BOM data showing NL rack-PDU revenue per colo MW in line with the CE average (e.g., a distributor split or Omdia country table showing NL PDU intensity ≈ DE's).","likelihood":{"value":0.3,"rationale":"The self-build concentration in NL is directly sourced (CBRE) and the low-PDU-content nature of self-build halls is well documented for AI-density builds; offsetting this, Amsterdam retains a large retail-colo and enterprise base, and Dutch grid constraints slow new self-build, limiting how fast the mix shifts within 12 months.","basis":"evidence"},"perturbation":[{"nodeId":"geo.NL","op":"set","value":0.21,"note":"NL slice smaller in absolute PDU-euro terms — self-build and hyperscale-leased MW discounted for lower PDU content; not a mix-shift to other countries"}],"indicators":[{"signal":"Rack-power architecture in new Amsterdam-region and North Holland build permits and fit-out tenders","where":"TenderNed (Dutch national tender portal); Dutch Data Center Association 'State of the Dutch Data Centers' annual report","threshold":"≥2 new NL builds specifying busway/shelf distribution as base design","updates":"increases"},{"signal":"NL share of European hyperscaler self-build pipeline","where":"CBRE European Data Centres quarterly; hyperscaler capex disclosures naming Dutch sites (Microsoft Middenmeer, Google Eemshaven expansions)","threshold":"NL self-build capacity additions exceeding NL colo additions in a calendar year","updates":"increases"}],"mitigations":[{"action":"Obtain a per-country PDU-intensity read (PDU revenue per colo MW for DE vs NL) via distributor interviews or an Omdia custom cut, replacing MW-only apportionment","type":"information","voiNodeId":"geo.NL","note":"Tests the euros-per-MW-constant assumption underlying the entire geo layer, not just NL"},{"action":"Weight Year-1 go-to-market toward Germany and Switzerland, whose capacity mix (colo/enterprise-heavy, self-operation-heavy) is structurally more PDU-native","type":"strategic","voiNodeId":null,"note":"Reallocates scarce pre-launch sales capacity away from the substitution-leading geography"}]},{"id":"large-operator-channel-forecloses-to-odm","title":"The chosen first buyer — large operators — is migrating PDU procurement into ODM whole-rack deals, moving the decision to a channel the venture doesn't sell through","narrative":"The venture's stated Year-1 route is selling to large operators, and cust.operator-large carries the biggest buyer weight (0.40) — justified in the ledger by hyperscale+colo being ~68% of capacity and large operators 'buying direct'. But direct purchase of discrete PDUs is exactly what large operators are unwinding: AI-density capacity is procured as integrated racks from ODM/rack integrators, where power distribution (shelf or PDU) is a BOM line the integrator sources under multi-year white-label agreements with incumbents. The demand doesn't shrink on paper — it relocates from cust.operator-large (where the venture has a channel plan) into cust.oem (where it has none), and BOM slots there are locked by Schneider/Vertiv/Eaton-class relationships years ahead. For a pre-launch entrant, a slice that moves to a foreclosed channel is functionally not addressable, whatever the buyer-mix pie chart says.","category":"competitive","targetNodes":["cust.operator-large"],"mechanism":[{"step":"trigger","text":"The venture's primary Year-1 buyer is large operators, whose capacity base (hyperscale 48% + colo 20% of worldwide capacity per Synergy end-2025) underpins the 0.40 weight — and whose new AI-density capacity is procured as ODM-integrated racks rather than component PDUs","refs":["profile:Who is the primary buyer you'll sell through first?","digest:seg-mix#c0","digest:seg-mix#c1","ledger:cust.operator-large"]},{"step":"propagation","text":"When rack power becomes an integrator BOM line, vendor selection shifts to white-label/OEM agreements dominated by the named incumbent set (Schneider at 15.8% plus a 5–7-vendor co-equal leader group per multiple reports); a pre-launch entrant with no ODM qualification cannot contest these slots inside 12 months","refs":["digest:cr3-vendors#c0","digest:cr3-vendors#c6","digest:cr3-vendors#c7","ledger:cust.oem"]},{"step":"model-impact","text":"cust.operator-large's 0.40 is a needs-source internal estimate resting on one misused company-level order stat with zero published channel data; if a material fraction of large-operator PDU spend flows through integrator channels, the direct-sale pool the venture's entire Year-1 motion targets is overstated — YAM's obtainable assumption is applied to a pool that partly is not there","refs":["ledger:cust.operator-large","ledger:cust.oem","ledger:obtainableFactor"]}],"whyMissable":"The buyer-mix nodes are flagged 'needs-source', so the team's instinct is to validate the percentages via interviews — checking whether 0.40 is the right number. The risk isn't the number; it's that the category boundary between 'operator buys direct' and 'operator buys a rack' is moving during the entry window, so even a correctly-measured 2025 split is wrong by the time the pipeline matures.","expectedObviousness":"low","falsifier":"Procurement documentation from 2–3 large CE operators showing intelligent rack PDUs still tendered as a discrete direct-purchase category (separate RFQ line, operator-selected vendor) for their 2026–27 capacity additions, including AI-density halls.","likelihood":{"value":0.35,"rationale":"Integrated-rack procurement is documented for hyperscale AI deployments and the buyer-mix nodes have no evidential floor to resist the shift; however, European colo operators still run discrete PDU RFQs for standard halls, and the pace of channel migration within a 12-month window is genuinely uncertain.","basis":"judgment"},"perturbation":[{"nodeId":"cust.operator-large","op":"set","value":0.28,"note":"Portion of large-operator PDU spend relocates into integrator BOMs foreclosed to a new entrant — absolute shrink of the directly-winnable pool, below the ledger's 0.33 band floor"}],"indicators":[{"signal":"Procurement structure in large-operator capacity programs (discrete PDU RFQs vs integrated rack packages)","where":"TED (EU tender platform) for operator framework agreements; Equinix/Digital Realty/NTT procurement supplier-onboarding portals and capex call commentary","threshold":"A major CE operator consolidating rack power into an integrated-rack or fit-out-contractor package for the first time","updates":"increases"},{"signal":"ODM/integrator rack-scale revenue and PDU white-label announcements","where":"OCP Global Summit sponsor/deployment talks;
